The morning always began the same way, with the low moan of wind pressing against the stone walls and the smell of salt finding its way through every crack and

tail: am in the old keeper's quarters. Thomas rose before the
sun, as he had every day for the past eleven years, swinging his legs over the side
of the narrow cot and feeling the cold of the floor through his wool
socks. He did not need an alarm. His body
had long since learned the rhythm of this place, tuned itself

to the turning of the light above and the shifting of the tides below.

He dressed in the half
-dark, pulling on the same heavy canvas tr
ousers and knit sweater he wore most days, and
crossed the small room to the stove. The fire had
gone to embers overnight, but a few pieces
of driftwood and a careful arrangement of kindling brought it back to life. He filled
the kettle from the rain cistern and set it on the iron plate, then stood
at the window while he waited for it to boil. The sea was a flat grey
sheet this morning, barely distinguishable from the sky. Somewhere out there, he knew
, fishing boats were already working the banks, but he could not see them.
On clear days he could sometimes count a dozen s

ails. Today there was only the grey.

The tea was
strong and bitter, the way he preferred it. He d
rank it standing, looking out at the rocks below the tower where
the waves spent themselves in lazy white curls. After breakfast, which
was porridge with a spoonful of honey from the last supply delivery, he clim
bed the spiral staircase to the lantern room. One hundred and twelve steps. He had counted
them once, years ago, and the number had stayed fixed in his mind ever since. The
Fresnel lens sat in its brass housing like some enormous
jewel, still turning slowly on its clockwork mechanism. He
checked the oil reservoir, trimmed the wick, and polished a few spots
on the glass where condensation had gathered overnight. The light would not
be needed again until dusk, but Thomas believed in read
iness. The sea did not wait for a keeper who

was behind schedule.

The rest of the morning he spent on maintenance. There
was always something that needed attention on an island this exposed to the elements. Today
it was the door to the storage shed, which had sw
ollen with moisture and refused to close properly. He planed it
down with a hand tool, working slowly and carefully, shaving
thin curls of wood onto the ground. A pair of oystercatchers watched
bright against the grey stone. He spoke to them occasionally, commenting on the weather or the stubbornness of the door

, and they regarded him with the patient indifference of creatures who had heard it all before.

In
the afternoon he walked the perimeter of the island, as he did most days when the weather
allowed. It was not a long walk, perhaps forty minutes if he
took his time, but it served a purpose beyond exercise. He checked
the shoreline for debris, looked for signs of erosion around the foundation of the tower, and noted the behavior
of the birds that nested along the cliffs on the eastern side. He kept a log
book of these observations, filling its pages with small, precise handwriting that recorded

wind direction, wave height, bird counts, and the occasional visit from a seal.

By
late afternoon the sky had begun to darken, not
with night but with weather. A band of cloud was
building to the west, and Thomas could feel the barometric pressure dropping in a
way that was less scientific measurement than bodily intuition. He clim
bed the stairs again, lit the lamp, and watched the beam sweep out across the water in
its steady, patient arc. Somewhere beyond the horizon, a ship
would see that light and correct its course, and the keeper and
the sailor would never know each other, connected only by a
thread of brightness cutting through the dark. Thomas stood at the glass
and watched the sea gather itself for the coming storm, and he felt, as he always
did at this hour, both entirely alone and entirely necessary.
